text: I begin by dividing the posterior fissure.
I perform this task by separating the two lobes along the oblique fissure and gently dissect down towards the pulmonary artery by providing tension by retracting the left upper lobe parenchyma.
The goal is to reach the avascular plane above the pulmonary artery.
Throughout the operation, it is important to only grasp and retract the portion of the lung being removed to avoid air leaks after surgery.
Once fissure dissection is complete, I proceed to dissect in the posterior mediastinum, starting just above the left main bronchus and exposing the pulmonary artery.
I use my left arm to help present the tissue while using the fenestrated bipolar instrument to divide the presented tissue.
During this dissection, I remove any mediastinal lymph nodes to facilitate the exposure of the pulmonary artery.
Next, I dissect on top of pulmonary artery to create a tunnel underneath the posterior fissure.
Once it's created, I place an endolator to help with the division of the fissure.
I connect the undulator to the robotic blue low stapler to guide the stapler around the tissue and divide the posterior fissure.
Next, I dissect the superior pulmonary vein to find all the branches leading to the apical trisegment.
Once the vein draining the apical trisegment is dissected out, I place the EndoLeader device around it.
I connect EndoLeader to the robotic white load stapler and guide it around the vein and divide the vein.
Next I dissect the tissue overlying the main pulmonary artery and identify the branches leading to the left upper lobe trisegment.
This is performed by dissecting on the avascular plane on top of the pulmonary artery.
Once the branch is dissected out,
I use the endoleader to help guide the robotic white load stapler around the pulmonary artery branch and to divide the branch.
I continue to dissect on the artery to expose the first branch of the main pulmonary artery leading to the apical trisegment.
Again, endoleader is instrumental in safely guiding the robotic white load stapler around the vessel.
Next, I dissect the apical trisegment branches of the left upper lobe bronchus.
I dissect out both the exit and entry point and place the endolete around the bronchus.
I use the endo leader to guide the green-low stapler around it to divide the airway.
To identify the parenchymal plane between the apical trisegment and lingular segment, I perform ICG angiography.
We inject 3 cc of ICG followed by 10 cc of saline flushed through the IV and turn the firefly feature on the robotic console.
This highlights the separation between the apical trisegment and lingular segment of the left upper lobe.
Since all the vasculature is divided to the apical trisegment, that segment remains black while the lingular segment lights up green.
I marked the border by bipolar on the lung parenchyma of the trisegment.
Using the marking, I divide the apical trisegment using the robotic blue low stapler.
I complete the segment technique by sequentially firing the blue low stapler to finalize the division.

This is how we perform the robot-assisted left upper lobe trisegmentectomy by utilizing the EndoLeader ICG guidance and robotic precision, we achieve an efficient and safe resection while preserving the lingular segment.
